 {"id":"ft-v5lz3.1.1","title":"[postswarm][mem-gc] Re-verify patterns_sweep_findings.md and GC if still clean (was STALE: 24 commits since 2ca20e18, threshold 5)","description":"# GC patterns_sweep_findings.md (re-verify or replace)

## Background
MEMORY.md self-tags patterns_sweep_findings.md as STALE:
"patterns.rs+pattern_trigger.rs cleanly hardened; 0 cat-A, 3 cat-B
candidates — STALE (24 commits since SHA 2ca20e18, threshold 5; tracked
under ft-hph8i / ft-nedq3)."

The original sweep found 0 cat-A (production stub/TODO/unimplemented
outside #[cfg(test)]) findings on patterns.rs and pattern_trigger.rs
at commit 2ca20e18. By 2026-04-27 the threshold was exceeded by 24
commits → automatically flagged STALE per the project's memory-
freshness convention.

## Why this matters
Carrying STALE memories in MEMORY.md inflates auto-loaded context
every session. The stale-tag mechanism only works if we actually GC
at the threshold; letting it slip teaches agents the threshold is
fictional.

## Scope
1. Re-run the original mock-finder sweep on patterns.rs and
   pattern_trigger.rs at HEAD.
2. If still 0 cat-A:
   - Delete /Users/jemanuel/.claude/projects/-Users-jemanuel-projects-frankenterm/memory/project_patterns_sweep_findings.md
   - Remove the patterns-subsystem-bug-sweep bullet line from MEMORY.md.
3. If new cat-A finding(s):
   - File a new audit bead (P1) with file:line + repro.
   - Update the MEMORY.md bullet to point at the new bead and advance
     the SHA + threshold.

## Out of scope
- Other memory entries (their own children handle them).
- Generalizing the GC threshold value — that's a meta-discussion.

## Approach
1. `git log --oneline 2ca20e18..HEAD -- crates/frankenterm-core/src/patterns.rs crates/frankenterm-core/src/pattern_trigger.rs`
   to surface what's changed since the threshold base. Read each commit
   message at minimum.
2. Re-apply the mock-finder method on the two files at HEAD:
   - `rg -n 'unimplemented!\(|todo!\(|panic!\("not implemented"' crates/frankenterm-core/src/patterns.rs crates/frankenterm-core/src/pattern_trigger.rs`
   - `rg -n 'FIXME|XXX|STUB|MOCK|FAKE|PLACEHOLDER|HACK' crates/frankenterm-core/src/patterns.rs crates/frankenterm-core/src/pattern_trigger.rs | grep -v '#\[cfg(test)\]'`
   - `rg -n '^\s+Ok\(\(\)\)\s*$' crates/frankenterm-core/src/patterns.rs crates/frankenterm-core/src/pattern_trigger.rs` → manually inspect any matches; flag only those that are production no-ops.
   - `rg -n 'mock_|fake_|stub_|dummy_' crates/frankenterm-core/src/patterns.rs crates/frankenterm-core/src/pattern_trigger.rs | grep -v '#\[cfg(test)\]'`
3. Decision branch:
   - 0 cat-A findings → GC path (steps 4a-4d).
   - >=1 cat-A finding → file path (steps 5a-5b).
4a. `rm /Users/jemanuel/.claude/projects/-Users-jemanuel-projects-frankenterm/memory/project_patterns_sweep_findings.md`
4b. Edit MEMORY.md: remove the line beginning "- [patterns-subsystem-bug-sweep-2026-04-20]".
4c. Verify: `cat MEMORY.md | grep patterns_sweep_findings` returns empty.
4d. Verify: `ls /Users/jemanuel/.claude/projects/-Users-jemanuel-projects-frankenterm/memory/project_patterns_sweep_findings.md` returns "no such file".
5a. `br create --type bug --priority 1 --title "[audit] patterns subsystem regression: <one-line>" --description "<file:line, repro, suggested fix>"`
5b. Edit MEMORY.md: replace the line with a pointer to the new bead and the new base-SHA + threshold.

## Acceptance criteria
- The stale entry is gone OR replaced with a current-finding pointer.
- A short comment is appended to this bead's description recording
  the decision (GC / new-finding) with the new-bead-id if applicable.

## Testing
- `cat /Users/jemanuel/.claude/projects/-Users-jemanuel-projects-frankenterm/memory/MEMORY.md | grep -E 'patterns_sweep_findings|patterns-subsystem-bug-sweep'` returns empty (or only points at the new audit bead).
- `ls /Users/jemanuel/.claude/projects/-Users-jemanuel-projects-frankenterm/memory/project_patterns_sweep_findings.md 2>&1 | grep -q "No such file"` exits 0 (in the GC branch).
- E2E logging: when running this bead, log each phase to stderr in JSON-line format: `{"phase":"git-log","files_changed":N}`, `{"phase":"rg-scan","matches":N}`, `{"phase":"decision","branch":"gc"|"file"}`, `{"phase":"action","files_removed":[...],"beads_filed":[...]}`. Output to /tmp/mem-gc-patterns-<timestamp>.log for post-hoc review.

## Risks / considerations
- AGENTS.md Rule 1 (no file deletion without permission) applies to
  REPO files; the memory directory is OUTSIDE the repo. The auto-memory
  rules in the system prompt explicitly contemplate "Update or remove
  memories that turn out to be wrong or outdated" — this is the
  authorized exception. Document this exception scope in the
  GC-action log entry.
- 24 commits is not a huge delta; the sweep should re-run quickly.
  If it doesn't finish in <5 min there's likely a methodology drift
  bug — pause and investigate before deleting.

## Labels
- postswarm-2026-04-27
- memory-gc
- chore

## References
- patterns.rs, pattern_trigger.rs in crates/frankenterm-core/src/
- ft-812ei (round-3 mock-finder sweep precedent)

 {"id":"ft-v5lz3.1.2","title":"[postswarm][mem-gc] Re-verify capture_storage_sweep_findings.md and GC if still clean (was STALE: 154 commits since f5a37ec9, threshold 30)","description":"# GC capture_storage_sweep_findings.md (re-verify or replace)

## Background
MEMORY.md self-tags capture_storage_sweep_findings.md as STALE:
"storage.rs+recorder_* cluster; 0 cat-A — STALE (154 commits since SHA
f5a37ec9, threshold 30; ft-hph8i / ft-nedq3)."

154 commits over the threshold of 30 is a 5x overshoot. The sweep
covered storage.rs and recorder_* files; high probability that the
landscape has shifted enough that the original "0 cat-A" claim no
longer holds, OR the files have been refactored such that the sweep
needs re-targeting.

## Why this matters
- 154 commits worth of drift is the largest of the three flagged
  stale memories. Either the audit is far out of date (high false-
  confidence risk) or there's no longer anything to audit (false
  burden on context).
- Storage/recorder is data-persistence-critical. A regressed audit
  here is more dangerous than the patterns one.

## Scope
1. Re-run the mock-finder sweep on storage.rs and recorder_* files
   at HEAD, with current file paths (verify storage.rs and
   recorder_* haven't been moved/renamed; if so, follow them).
2. If still 0 cat-A:
   - Delete /Users/jemanuel/.claude/projects/-Users-jemanuel-projects-frankenterm/memory/project_capture_storage_sweep_findings.md
   - Remove the capture-storage-subsystem-bug-sweep bullet line from
     MEMORY.md.
3. If new cat-A finding(s):
   - File new P1 audit bead(s).
   - Update the MEMORY.md bullet to point at the new bead and advance
     SHA + threshold.

## Out of scope
- Other memory entries.

## Approach
1. Confirm file paths still exist:
   - `find crates -name "storage.rs" -path "*/frankenterm-core*"` →
     expect crates/frankenterm-core/src/storage.rs.
   - `find crates -name "recorder_*.rs" -path "*/frankenterm-core*"` →
     expect a cluster of recorder_lexical, recorder_search, etc.
   - If any path is gone, note in the decision log and adjust scope.
2. `git log --oneline f5a37ec9..HEAD -- <file paths from step 1>`
   (only filter shows what's actually changed; expect a long list).
3. Re-apply the mock-finder method on each file:
   - `rg -n 'unimplemented!\(|todo!\(|panic!\("not implemented"' <files>`
   - `rg -n 'FIXME|XXX|STUB|MOCK|FAKE|PLACEHOLDER|HACK' <files> | grep -v '#\[cfg(test)\]'`
   - Manually inspect any `Ok(())` returning trivial values in
     production functions that look like stubs.
   - `rg -n 'mock_|fake_|stub_|dummy_' <files> | grep -v '#\[cfg(test)\]'`
4. Decision branch + action (same shape as the patterns child).
5. Memory-update steps mirror the patterns child.

## Acceptance criteria
- Stale entry is gone OR replaced with a current pointer.
- Decision log appended to this bead's description.

## Testing
- Verification commands mirror the patterns child but for the
  capture-storage memory file.
- E2E logging requirement: same JSON-line schema as patterns child.
  Output to /tmp/mem-gc-capstore-<timestamp>.log.
- Coverage gate: the rg commands must collectively cover all current
  storage and recorder files; if a new recorder_*.rs file has been
  added since f5a37ec9, it must be in the sweep set.

## Risks / considerations
- 154 commits is a HUGE delta. If the audit finds even one new cat-A,
  the decision tree must NOT GC; instead, file the new bead and update
  the memory pointer.
- Don't bundle multiple findings into one bead — one bead per finding,
  each independently claimable.

## Labels
- postswarm-2026-04-27
- memory-gc
- chore

## References
- storage.rs, recorder_*.rs in crates/frankenterm-core/src/
- ft-812ei (round-3 mock-finder sweep precedent)

 {"id":"ft-v5lz3.1.3","title":"[postswarm][mem-gc] Re-verify mux_term_codec_sweep.md and GC if still clean (was STALE: 91 commits since 312f06b9, threshold 20)","description":"# GC mux_term_codec_sweep.md (re-verify or replace)

## Background
MEMORY.md self-tags mux_term_codec_sweep.md as STALE:
"0 production stubs; ft-yv02l's 29 unimplemented!() were FakePane
#[cfg(test)] — STALE (91 commits since SHA 312f06b9, threshold 20;
ft-hph8i / ft-nedq3)."

The original sweep covered the in-tree mux/term/codec subsystems
(frankenterm/mux/, frankenterm/term/, frankenterm/codec/) and
correctly distinguished 29 unimplemented!() in FakePane (which are
guarded by #[cfg(test)]) from production stubs. 91 commits past the
threshold of 20 is a 4.5x overshoot.

## Why this matters
The mux/term/codec subsystems are the core terminal-emulator surface
inherited from the wezterm fork (in-tree as frankenterm/<crate>/).
A new production stub here would be a real-world rendering or
pty-IO regression risk.

## Scope
1. Re-run the sweep at HEAD across frankenterm/mux/**/*.rs,
   frankenterm/term/**/*.rs, frankenterm/codec/**/*.rs with the
   FakePane-aware filter (cfg(test) excluded).
2. If still 0 production stubs:
   - Delete /Users/jemanuel/.claude/projects/-Users-jemanuel-projects-frankenterm/memory/project_mux_term_codec_sweep.md
   - Remove the mux-term-codec-mock-finder-sweep bullet line from
     MEMORY.md.
3. If new production stub(s):
   - File new P1 audit bead(s).
   - Update the MEMORY.md bullet pointer + advance SHA + threshold.

## Out of scope
- Other memory entries.
- Refactoring decisions about FakePane (it's a test-only doppelganger
  by design — see ft-yv02l for context).

## Approach
1. Verify file roots still exist:
   - `find frankenterm -type d -name mux -o -name term -o -name codec`
2. `git log --oneline 312f06b9..HEAD -- frankenterm/mux frankenterm/term frankenterm/codec`
3. Re-apply mock-finder with FakePane-aware filter:
   - `rg -n 'unimplemented!\(|todo!\(|panic!\("not implemented"' frankenterm/mux frankenterm/term frankenterm/codec --type rust | grep -v 'FakePane' | grep -v '#\[cfg(test)\]'`
   - `rg -n 'FIXME|XXX|STUB|MOCK|FAKE|PLACEHOLDER|HACK' frankenterm/mux frankenterm/term frankenterm/codec --type rust | grep -v 'FakePane' | grep -v '#\[cfg(test)\]'`
   - Manual inspection of `Ok(())` and trivial returns in any function
     marked `pub` outside test gates.
4. Decision branch + action mirror the patterns child.

## Acceptance criteria
- Stale entry is gone OR replaced with a current pointer.
- Decision log appended to this bead's description.
- Any new audit bead explicitly notes that FakePane is intentionally
  unimplemented (test-only) and excludes it.

## Testing
- Verification commands mirror the patterns child but scoped to
  mux/term/codec.
- E2E logging: same JSON-line schema. Output to
  /tmp/mem-gc-muxtermcodec-<timestamp>.log.
- FakePane regression check: a `rg` baseline at 312f06b9 vs HEAD
  must show that FakePane is the same shape (still `#[cfg(test)]`)
  before declaring the sweep clean.

## Risks / considerations
- The vendored frankenterm/<crate>/ tree is the wezterm fork; it has
  its own conventions. Don't pattern-match this audit against
  frankenterm-core/.
- 91 commits is large but not as severe as capture-storage (154).
  Still: re-run carefully, do not skip the FakePane filter.

## Labels
- postswarm-2026-04-27
- memory-gc
- chore

- frankenterm/mux, frankenterm/term, frankenterm/codec (in-tree fork)
- ft-yv02l (FakePane context)

-{"id":"ft-v5lz3.1.4","title":"[postswarm][mem-gc][stretch] Automate memory-staleness checks: monthly cron emits a list of past-threshold memories","description":"# Automate memory-staleness checks

## Background
The 2026-04-27 swarm flagged 3 STALE subsystem-sweep memories that had
been past their freshness threshold for weeks. The stale-tag mechanism
relied on humans noticing — by 24/154/91 commits past threshold, the
self-tag had been ignored long enough that all three needed manual
inventory.

This bead automates the staleness check so future memories don't drift.

## Why this matters
- Manual staleness audits don't scale; automation makes them reliable.
- Memory-budget hygiene is silent until it's a problem (context cost
  doesn't show up as an error).
- A cron-style emitter that produces an actionable list in chat or
  email keeps the loop closed.

## Scope (P4, stretch — not blocking)
1. Author a small script (scripts/memory-staleness-check.sh or .py)
   that:
   - Reads the user's auto-memory MEMORY.md.
   - For each entry that includes a "STALE (... commits since SHA X,
     threshold T)" pattern, checks if the current commit count from
     X is >= T.
   - Emits a JSON-line list of entries that exceed threshold.
2. Schedule the script: cron, launchd, or `/loop`-style scheduled
   prompt. Recommend `/loop` since it's already swarm-native.
3. Output destination: a markdown summary file checked into
   docs/memory-staleness-report.md OR a chat message OR a generated
   bead.

## Out of scope
- Auto-GC'ing stale entries (humans should still review).
- Memory-system improvements unrelated to staleness.

## Approach
1. Read MEMORY.md, parse STALE entries via regex.
2. For each: `git log --oneline <SHA>..HEAD | wc -l` to get commit count.
3. Compare to threshold; emit if exceeded.
4. Schedule via `/schedule` skill (recurring bead, e.g., monthly).
5. Or wire as a /loop dynamic-mode auto-bead generator.

## Acceptance criteria
- Script exists and emits the expected JSON-line shape.
- A test fixture with mocked MEMORY.md verifies parsing.
- Scheduled (via /schedule or /loop) at a documented cadence.
- A first run produces zero false positives (verify against current
  MEMORY.md state after the 3 GC'd entries).

## Testing
- Unit: feed mocked MEMORY.md with known-stale + known-fresh entries;
  assert classification.
- Integration: run on actual MEMORY.md after GC bead closes; assert
  empty output.
- E2E logging: JSON-line format with fields {entry_id, sha, threshold,
  current_commit_count, stale: bool}.

## Risks / considerations
- Don't over-engineer. A 30-line script is plenty.
- Cron-style automation can flake; treat the report as advisory, not
  blocking.
- Memory entry format (pinned-SHA + threshold) needs to stay
  consistent. Document the expected format in MEMORY.md's header.

## Labels
- postswarm-2026-04-27
- memory-gc
- automation
- stretch

## References
- MEMGC siblings (.1.1, .1.2, .1.3) — the memories this would have
  flagged earlier.

 {"id":"ft-v5lz3.2","title":"[postswarm] Harden operator helpers: tests + shellcheck + runbook + optional ntm-coordinator integration","description":"# Operator-helper hardening — root

## Background
During the 2026-04-27 swarm, the orchestrator authored two ad-hoc
operator scripts and committed them as 3fee5c13:

- scripts/swarm-tick.sh — reads `br`, `git log`, `df`, `ntm
  --robot-status`, and per-pane tails to emit a single JSON snapshot
  the orchestrator can consume each tick.
- scripts/clean-stale-targets.sh — removes /tmp/ft-*-target dirs older
  than N hours; reports KB freed.

Both ran reliably under load during 16 ticks but neither has tests,
shellcheck CI gates, or an operator runbook. Both are now part of the
project's operator surface and will be reused by every future swarm —
hardening them is a one-time payoff that compounds.

## Why this matters (overarching project goals)
- frankenterm dogfoods itself: the same scripts that operate the swarm
  developing frankenterm will also operate any swarm running on
  frankenterm-as-a-product. A regression in the operator layer makes
  every future swarm session worse.
- Disk-pressure incidents during the session climbed to 96% before
  agents self-cleaned via clean-stale-targets.sh. A bug in that script
  could either (a) miss stale dirs (silent disk fill) or (b) eat live
  builds (silent regression in the swarm). Tests prevent both.
- Operator runbook bridges the agents/humans gap — anyone (next-session
  agent or a teammate) should be able to run the operator loop without
  reading 16 ticks of chat history.

## Scope (this is the ROOT epic with five children)
1. Unit + golden tests for swarm-tick.sh (mocked br/git/df/ntm fixtures).
2. Unit tests for clean-stale-targets.sh (dry-run mode + threshold
   coverage + edge cases).
3. shellcheck CI gate for scripts/*.sh (so future operator-helper
   regressions are caught before merge).
4. Operator runbook doc (docs/operator-runbook.md) — the playbook for
   running a swarm tick-by-tick.
5. (Stretch, P3) ntm-coordinator integration so the operator agent
   can be replaced by `ntm coordinator enable` when desired.

## Out of scope
- Rewriting either script in another language.
- New operator features beyond what the session validated.
- Production logging/observability (file as separate bead if needed).

## Definition of done (root)
- All children closed.
- `git log --oneline scripts/` shows only intentional changes (no
  reverts of 3fee5c13 work).
- `cargo test` (or equivalent) green on the new test scaffolding.
- shellcheck runs clean across scripts/*.sh.
- A new contributor can read docs/operator-runbook.md and drive a
  swarm tick without further context.

## Testing
Each child has explicit unit + e2e test specifications. The e2e tests
must emit structured JSON-line logs (one event per phase) so that
post-failure log inspection is mechanical.

## Risks / considerations
- bash testing is fiddly. Use a thin BATS-style harness or a Rust
  integration test that shells out — pick whichever is already wired.
- Don't over-test the scripts to the point that small changes need
  many test updates. Focus on golden-output tests for the JSON snapshot
  shape and on threshold/dry-run boundary tests for cleanup.
- shellcheck CI gate must NOT block on style-only warnings; pin the
  rule set to error-class only.

 {"id":"ft-v5lz3.2.1","title":"[postswarm][ops] Unit + golden tests for scripts/swarm-tick.sh (mocked br/git/df/ntm fixtures)","description":"# Unit + golden tests for scripts/swarm-tick.sh

## Background
scripts/swarm-tick.sh was authored ad-hoc during the 2026-04-27 swarm
session and committed in 3fee5c13. It runs every 4 minutes (or
on-demand) and emits a single JSON snapshot the orchestrator agent
consumes to decide which panes need fresh marching orders, whether
disk needs cleanup, whether convergence has held, etc.

Inputs the script reads:
- `git log --since "1 hour ago"` and `--since "4 minutes ago"`
- `br list --status open|in_progress|blocked --json` and `br ready --json`
- `df -h /System/Volumes/Data`
- `find /tmp -maxdepth 1 -name "ft-*-target" -mmin +720`
- `ls -d /tmp/ft-*-target` and `du -sk /tmp/ft-*-target`
- `ntm --robot-status` (reads sessions, agents, panes)

Output: JSON with shape (line ~80 of swarm-tick.sh):
{
  "ts": "<rfc3339>",
  "session": "<name>",
  "git": { "commits_1h": N, "commits_since_last_tick": N },
  "beads": { "open": N, "in_progress": N, "blocked": N, "ready": N },
  "disk": { "data_avail": "<X>Gi", "data_used_pct": "<Y>%",
            "stale_targets_12h": N, "total_targets": N,
            "targets_size_mb": MB },
  "swarm": { "panes_count": N, "agents": [...] }
}

## Why this matters
The orchestrator depends on this JSON shape being stable. A regression
in swarm-tick.sh — either a new field that breaks downstream parsing
or a missing field — silently degrades every future orchestrator
session. Tests prevent silent regressions.

## Scope
Test infrastructure for scripts/swarm-tick.sh:
1. Mock-fixture harness: tests/fixtures/swarm-tick/ with mocked
   responses for `br list --json`, `br ready --json`,
   `ntm --robot-status`, `git log`, `df`, `du`, etc.
2. Golden snapshot tests: feed each fixture set through swarm-tick.sh
   and assert the JSON output matches a checked-in golden.
3. Boundary/edge tests:
   - Zero panes / zero beads / empty fixture set.
   - All fields present vs missing optional fields.
   - Disk just under / just over 96% threshold.
   - Total targets = 0 and total targets > 10.
   - `commits_since_last_tick` boundary (commits in last 4 min).
4. Test runner integration: hook into the project's existing test
   runner (likely a bash-based BATS suite under tests/, or a Rust
   integration test that shells out via `std::process::Command`).
5. Detailed JSON-line logging during test execution, so failures
   produce immediately actionable output.

## Out of scope
- Refactoring swarm-tick.sh internals.
- Testing the orchestrator agent's CONSUMPTION of the JSON (that's a
  separate concern; this bead tests producer correctness only).

## Approach
1. Decide test framework: prefer BATS if no Rust harness for shell
   exists yet; use `cargo test` integration test with `Command::new`
   if there's existing precedent in tests/.
2. Build mock fixtures:
   - tests/fixtures/swarm-tick/healthy/   — typical 6-pane swarm,
     2 ready beads, 95% disk.
   - tests/fixtures/swarm-tick/empty/     — no session, no beads.
   - tests/fixtures/swarm-tick/disk-pressure/ — 96% used, 7 active dirs.
   - tests/fixtures/swarm-tick/converged/ — 0 ready, 0 open.
   - Each fixture directory contains:
     - br_open.json, br_in_progress.json, br_blocked.json, br_ready.json
     - ntm_robot_status.json
     - git_log_1h.txt, git_log_4m.txt
     - df_h.txt
     - find_targets.txt, du_targets.txt
     - expected.json — the golden output.
3. Write a thin shim in tests/swarm-tick-runner.sh that exports
   stub commands (br/ntm/git/df/du) onto PATH so swarm-tick.sh reads
   the fixtures instead of live state.
4. Implement test cases in tests/swarm_tick_tests.sh (BATS) or
   tests/swarm_tick_test.rs (Rust integration test).
5. Each test logs JSON-line events: {"phase":"setup","fixture":"X"},
   {"phase":"run","exit_code":0}, {"phase":"compare","matches":true|false},
   {"phase":"diff","actual_vs_expected":"<unified-diff>"}.

## Acceptance criteria
- ≥4 fixture sets covered (healthy, empty, disk-pressure, converged).
- Each fixture has a checked-in expected.json golden.
- All tests pass via `cargo test --test swarm_tick` or
  `bats tests/swarm_tick_tests.sh`.
- Tests run in CI (via the shellcheck-CI bead, which sets up the
  shell-test lane).
- A regression in swarm-tick.sh that changes any documented field is
  caught by at least one test.
- Test execution emits JSON-line logs to stderr for inspection.

## Testing (THIS bead is itself a test bead, but it has its own meta-tests)
- Verify by INDUCING a regression: temporarily change swarm-tick.sh to
  miss the "stale_targets_12h" field; run the test suite; confirm at
  least one test fails with a clear diff. Then revert.
- Verify by INDUCING a fixture mismatch: corrupt one fixture's
  expected.json; confirm the test fails clearly. Then revert.
- Run the full suite under `time` and verify <5s wall time on
  developer laptop.

## Risks / considerations
- Stubbing `br` and `ntm` requires careful PATH manipulation to avoid
  test bleed-through. Use a hermetic test directory.
- Don't test against live state — that's flaky and racy with the
  swarm itself.
- shellcheck must pass on the fixture-runner shim.

- postswarm-2026-04-27
- operator-tooling
- test

 {"id":"ft-v5lz3.2.2","title":"[postswarm][ops] Unit tests for scripts/clean-stale-targets.sh (dry-run + threshold + edge cases)","description":"# Unit tests for scripts/clean-stale-targets.sh

## Background
scripts/clean-stale-targets.sh was authored ad-hoc during the
2026-04-27 swarm and committed in 3fee5c13. It removes
/tmp/ft-*-target dirs older than N hours (default 12) and reports KB
freed. During the session it correctly cleaned 6 dirs / ~302MB at
the 12h threshold, and 0 dirs at 6h (because they were live builds).

A bug in this script could either (a) miss stale dirs (silent disk
fill, eventually causing the swarm to fail mid-tick) or (b) eat live
builds (silent regression; the affected agent's build cache is gone
and they re-do work). Tests prevent both.

## Why this matters
- Disk pressure during the 2026-04-27 session climbed to 96% before
  agents self-cleaned. A regressed cleanup script would let it climb
  to 100% and stall the swarm.
- The script uses `stat -f %m` (BSD/macOS) for mtime — Linux uses
  `stat -c %Y`. This script is currently macOS-only. Tests must
  document that and gate Linux runs.

## Scope
Test infrastructure for scripts/clean-stale-targets.sh:
1. Mock target-dir layout in a test temp directory:
   - tests/fixtures/clean-stale/setup.sh — creates fake /tmp/ft-*-
     target dirs with controlled mtimes via `touch -t YYYYMMDDhhmm`.
2. Test cases:
   - All dirs newer than threshold → 0 removed.
   - All dirs older than threshold → all removed.
   - Mixed → only old dirs removed.
   - No dirs match the glob → exit 0, 0 removed, no error.
   - Empty dir vs non-empty dir behavior.
   - Threshold=0 (everything stale).
   - Very large threshold (nothing stale, even old dirs).
3. Dry-run mode: if the script doesn't have one, ADD a `--dry-run`
   flag in this same bead — print "would remove X" without removing.
   This is a small addition that makes testing dramatically safer.
4. Error path coverage:
   - Permission denied on a target dir.
   - Race: dir disappears between find and rm.
5. Detailed JSON-line logging during test execution.

## Out of scope
- Cleaning anything OTHER than /tmp/ft-*-target.
- Adding scheduling (cron/launchd) — that's covered by ntm-integration.

## Approach
1. Add `--dry-run` flag to clean-stale-targets.sh:
   - Reads `--dry-run` arg or `DRY_RUN=1` env var.
   - In dry-run mode, prints `would-remove $d (age=${age_min}m)`
     instead of `removed $d`.
   - Exit code 0 in both modes when nothing went wrong.
2. Build the test fixture harness:
   - Use a temp directory pattern: `mktemp -d /tmp/clean-stale-test-XXXX`
   - Override TARGET_GLOB inside the script (export-driven) so tests
     can target the temp dir instead of real /tmp/ft-*-target.
3. Write tests in tests/clean_stale_tests.sh (BATS) covering each
   scenario above.
4. Run shellcheck on the modified script.
5. Each test logs JSON-line events to stderr.

## Acceptance criteria
- `--dry-run` flag is wired and documented in the script header.
- ≥7 test cases covering boundary behavior.
- All tests pass via `bats tests/clean_stale_tests.sh`.
- Tests run in CI (via the shellcheck-CI bead).
- shellcheck on clean-stale-targets.sh passes.

## Testing (the test-bead's meta-test)
- Induce regression: temporarily change the threshold check from `>`
  to `>=`; run tests; confirm boundary case fails. Revert.
- Run with set-x mode and capture full trace; verify each test step
  is logged.
- macOS-only sanity: skip Linux runs with a `[[ $(uname) == Darwin ]]`
  guard at top of script (or test-only guard with `# linux: skip`).
- Performance: full suite <2s.

## Risks / considerations
- Real /tmp/ft-*-target dirs must NOT be touched by tests. Use a
  hermetic test directory and never let the test path reach /tmp/ft-.
- The script currently uses `stat -f %m` (BSD). If we want Linux
  compatibility (some CI runs on Linux), wrap stat invocation in a
  uname check OR use perl/python for portable mtime — file as a
  follow-on if not in scope here.
- AGENTS.md Rule 1 (no file deletion without permission). The script
  IS the deletion mechanism — but only for /tmp/ft-*-target directories
  which are explicitly per-agent build caches, not project repo files.
  Document this exception in the script header and the test fixture
  README.

### --dry-run exit-code contract (explicit)
- Exit 0: dry-run completed; report what would have been removed.
- Exit 1: would have removed nothing AND there were no candidates
  (vacuously fine).
- Exit 2: dry-run encountered an error (e.g., glob expansion
  failed). Real-mode counterparts must match.
- Exit code is INDEPENDENT of how many dirs would-have-been-
  removed. A dry-run that reports "would-remove 5 dirs" still
  exits 0.

### Output format in dry-run mode
```
[dry-run] would-remove /tmp/ft-swarm-X-target (age=850m)
[dry-run] would-remove /tmp/ft-swarm-Y-target (age=720m)
[dry-run] cleaned 0 dirs (would have cleaned 2); KB before=12345 after=12345
```
The trailing summary line must say "cleaned 0 dirs" so downstream
parsers (swarm-tick.sh) don't double-count.

### Log cardinality / rotation
BATS suite tests can produce O(N_fixtures × N_phases) JSON-line
events. For OT_CLEAN's BATS suite this is small (~20 events per
test, 7 tests = 140 lines max). No rotation needed.
For OT_TICK and GPU harness e2e, log volume is larger; cap at
1000 lines per run by default; rotate via `> /tmp/.../run.log
2> /tmp/.../err.log` and let CI artifact-upload handle archival.

### Lock-file invariant (cross-ref to OT_CONCURRENCY)
After OT_CONCURRENCY (ft-v5lz3.2.8) lands, this bead's tests must
also verify lock-file behavior: dry-run does NOT acquire the

 {"id":"wa-nu4.3.8.3","title":"Sync push/pull for config + binary (explicit, non-destructive)","description":"# Task: Sync push/pull for config + binary (feature sync)

## Goal
Implement the core sync operations for:
- `~/.config/wa/**` (config)
- optional `wa` binary (explicit opt-in)

The intent is to make it easy to keep multiple machines consistent **without ever surprising the user**.

## Design principles
- **Explicitness over magic**: no background sync; only `push`/`pull`.
- **Plan-first**: always compute and display a plan; require `--apply` for real changes.
- **No silent overwrites**: overwriting requires confirmation and clear diff metadata.
- **Secret-aware**: never sync forbidden paths; never print secret contents.

## Path selection rules
- Config root is the canonical wa config directory.
- Include only known-safe config artifacts.
- Exclude patterns like:
  - `**/.env*`
  - `**/*token*`
  - `**/*secret*`
  - keychains/SSH keys

(Exact allow/deny rules are authored in the sync spec bead; this bead implements them.)

## Conflict semantics
When both sides changed:
- classify as `Conflict` and refuse to apply automatically
- provide remediation:
  - show which file conflicted
  - suggest manual resolution steps
  - (future) offer an interactive merge/choose-side mode

## Binary sync
- Treat binary sync as a separate category:
  - opt-in in config
  - prefer syncing from a trusted build machine to others
- Validate binary integrity (hash/size) before replacing.

## Testing
- Covered by `wa-nu4.3.8.5` (sync tests):
  - offline push/pull planning and dry-run output stability
  - conflict classification (no silent overwrite)
  - secret/path deny rules

## Acceptance Criteria
- `wa sync push/pull` computes a deterministic plan for config + binary categories.
- `--dry-run` never modifies local or remote files.
- Applying changes requires `--apply` and refuses to overwrite without explicit confirmation.
- Conflicts are detected and surfaced as `Conflict` items (no auto-overwrite).

 {"id":"wa-nu4.3.8.4","title":"Sync exported DB snapshots (immutable artifacts, versioned filenames)","description":"# Task: Sync exported DB snapshots (immutable artifacts)

## Goal
Make it easy to move **database snapshots** between machines for debugging or continuity, without ever touching the live DB file.

## Safety model
- Only sync snapshots created via `wa export` / `wa diag bundle` (explicit export paths).
- Never sync the live DB file or WAL/SHM.
- Treat snapshots as immutable artifacts:
  - versioned filenames
  - no in-place overwrite
  - optional compression (if/when supported by export)

## Naming & versioning
- Filenames should encode enough metadata to be self-describing:
  - wa version
  - created timestamp (UTC)
  - workspace key (hashed/short)
  - optional host

## Push/pull behavior
- `push` uploads snapshot(s) to the target under an artifacts directory.
- `pull` downloads snapshots and places them under a local artifacts directory.
- Default behavior is additive (no overwrites).

## UX expectations
- Plans should clearly distinguish:
  - “syncing snapshots” (safe)
  - “syncing live DB” (forbidden; should never appear)

## Testing
- Covered by `wa-nu4.3.8.5` (sync tests):
  - live DB paths are rejected
  - snapshot filenames are stable and never overwritten
  - push/pull round-trips via local-directory backend

## Acceptance Criteria
- `wa sync push` can push an exported snapshot to a target without overwriting existing files.
- `wa sync pull` can pull the same snapshot back and preserves the filename.
- Attempts to sync live DB paths are rejected by the planner.

 {"id":"wa-nu4.3.8.5","title":"Sync tests: config diffing + dry-run output stability (feature sync)","description":"# Task: Sync tests (feature sync)

## Goal
Make `wa sync` safe and boring by locking down:
- **path selection rules** (what may sync vs must never sync)
- **plan generation** (what would happen)
- **dry-run output stability** (humans + automation can trust it)

Sync is inherently risky: it touches configs and binaries, and can easily become a secret-exfiltration or accidental-overwrite footgun. Tests are the safety net.

## Scope
- Unit + integration tests that run **offline** and **deterministically**.
- No real SSH, no real remote machines, no real secrets.

## Test strategy
### 1) Pure unit tests (no filesystem required)
- **Path allow/deny rules**
  - allow: `~/.config/wa/**` (except explicit denylist)
  - allow (optional): `wa` binary (explicit opt-in)
  - deny: any known secret file patterns (tokens, keyrings, `.env`, etc.)
- **Target resolution**
  - parse configured sync targets
  - explicit “default target” selection rules
  - stable ordering (targets and files are sorted deterministically)
- **Planning semantics**
  - classify changes as `Add` / `Update` / `Skip` / `Conflict`
  - ensure “would overwrite” requires an explicit confirmation gate

### 2) Filesystem integration tests (temp dirs)
- Build a temp “local” tree and a temp “remote” tree (local-directory transport backend).
- Exercise:
  - `push --dry-run` produces a plan but changes nothing
  - `pull --dry-run` produces a plan but changes nothing
  - “same content” yields a minimal/no-op plan
  - “different content” yields Update items (but still requires confirmation in non-dry-run)

### 3) Golden tests for dry-run output stability
- Snapshot the **dry-run plan output** (both modes):
  - TTY-ish human format (no ANSI in tests; use the plain renderer)
  - machine JSON format (stable schema + stable ordering)
- The golden tests should assert:
  - stable ordering of files/targets
  - stable wording for safety warnings
  - redaction is applied to any values that might contain secrets

## Logging & debuggability requirements
- Tests should capture and assert a few critical log lines:
  - which target selected
  - how many items in plan
  - whether an overwrite would have occurred
- Log output must never include file contents or secret values.

## Acceptance Criteria
- `cargo test --features sync` includes deterministic tests that cover:
  - path selection allow/deny rules (including a “secret-ish” fixture)
  - plan generation for push + pull (no-op, update, conflict)
  - dry-run output stability (goldens/snapshots)
- Tests are fully offline (no SSH/network), fast, and reproducible.
- A regression that would accidentally sync a forbidden path fails loudly.


## Testing
- Meta-validation:

## Mission
Make v0.1.0 shippable:
- clear docs
- reliable CI signals
- predictable version/build metadata

## Why
A tool that controls terminals needs high trust and clear operator UX.

## Definition of done
- `cargo fmt --check`, `cargo clippy -- -D warnings`, `cargo test` pass in CI.
- README + quickstart lets another engineer install and run wa.
- Version/build metadata is visible (`wa --version`).

## Success Criteria
- CI runs fmt/clippy/tests/e2e reliably and preserves artifacts on failure.
- Release workflow/versioning produces reproducible binaries with build metadata.
- Docs/README/quickstart are sufficient for a new contributor to install and run wa.

## Testing
- CI verification:
  - CI must run the full quality gate (fmt/clippy/tests) and E2E, and upload artifacts on failure.

- Release smoke tests:
  - After packaging, run `wa --version` and `wa --help` and a minimal `wa doctor --format json` scenario to ensure the binary is functional.

 {"id":"wa-nu4.3.9.1","title":"CI pipeline: fmt/clippy/test/bench (feature matrix for mcp/web/tui/sync/browser)","description":"# Task: CI pipeline

## Goal
Ensure core quality checks run automatically on changes and failures are diagnosable from logs/artifacts.

## Core checks (blocking)
- `cargo fmt --check`
- `cargo clippy --all-targets -- -D warnings`
- `cargo test`

## Feature matrix smoke (blocking or warn-only; decide explicitly)
Build/test the crate under common feature combinations so optional integrations don’t silently rot.

Baseline matrix (PLAN Appendix A/F driven):
- default features
- `--features mcp`
- `--features web`
- `--features tui`
- `--features sync`
- `--features browser`

Vendored lane (PLAN Appendix H):
- When `--features vendored` exists, CI must at least:
  - `cargo check --all-targets --features vendored`
  - and run a minimal smoke/unit subset that exercises vendored wiring

This is intentionally separated from the ROI decision (`wa-nu4.4.1.6`): once vendored is enabled, CI must keep it from rotting.

## Docs correctness lane
- Add a docs-smoke step/job that keeps README quickstart commands executable (tracked by `wa-nu4.3.9.9`).

## Related CI jobs (separate tasks)
- E2E job for real WezTerm scenarios: `wa-nu4.3.9.6`
- Setup idempotency job (safe dotfile patching in temp home): `wa-nu4.3.9.8`
- Vendored maintenance lane: `wa-nu4.4.1.7`

## Artifacts and logging
- On failure, upload:
  - test logs (stdout/stderr)
  - E2E artifacts (runner logs + captured outputs)
  - benchmark outputs when applicable

## Testing
- CI “tests the tests”:
  - Add a minimal job that prints the exact rustc/cargo versions and the exact feature-matrix commands being executed.
  - Ensure CI captures and uploads logs/artifacts for failing jobs so failures are actionable.

## Acceptance Criteria
- CI failures are actionable (clear logs, no flaky tests).
- Optional features (especially vendored) do not silently break over time.

 {"id":"wa-nu4.3.9.10","title":"Beads hygiene: eliminate bd lint template warnings (Acceptance Criteria headings, missing sections)","description":"# Task: Beads hygiene (template lint to zero)

## Goal
Make the Beads plan itself reliable and self-documenting by ensuring bd lint produces zero template warnings.

This improves:
- onboarding for future contributors
- clarity during implementation
- confidence that tasks have explicit acceptance criteria

## Scope
- For all open tasks/features/bugs:
  - ensure the description includes the required section heading(s):
    - tasks/features: ## Acceptance Criteria
    - bugs: ## Steps to Reproduce, ## Acceptance Criteria
  - ensure the acceptance criteria are concrete and testable

## Change discipline
- Do not delete or truncate existing content.
- Prefer minimal edits:
  - rename "## Acceptance" -> "## Acceptance Criteria"
  - add a missing section at the end if absent

## Acceptance Criteria
- bd lint --status open produces no warnings.
- New issues created in this project follow the template by default.


## Testing
- Run `bd lint` locally after any template edits.
- Add a simple CI check (if desired) that runs `bd lint --status open` and fails on warnings.

 {"id":"wa-nu4.3.9.11","title":"CI: shellcheck install.sh + scripts/install-hooks.sh (ship-gate for installer shell)","description":"## Gap

Ship-readiness epic wa-nu4.3.9 covers fmt/clippy/tests/release packaging, but the **public installer shell** (`install.sh` at repo root, the `curl | bash` one-liner target) and `scripts/install-hooks.sh` have zero CI coverage. A subtle bash bug there would break every fresh user install without CI catching it.

## Deliverable

Add a lightweight `installer-shell` job to `.github/workflows/ci.yml` that runs on Ubuntu and:

- `bash -n` syntax-checks `install.sh` and `scripts/install-hooks.sh`
- `shellcheck` (error+warning severity) against both scripts
- Fails the build on any finding

## Why not scripts/*.sh broadly

`scripts/*.sh` is a large internal surface with known style noise; a strict shellcheck pass there is a separate triage task. This bead is scoped to the two scripts that run on end-user machines outside our dev env, where a break = broken install day one.

## Acceptance

- New job visible in Actions tab
- Runs in < 60s (no Rust build)
- Both scripts parse clean under `bash -n`; both pass shellcheck at severity `warning`
- Part of the required ship-readiness gate (no `continue-on-error`)

 {"id":"wa-nu4.3.9.19","title":"[follow-up ft-tczj7] Find serde-reject slip payload that reaches FT-MCP-0001 tool envelope","description":"## Context

ft-tczj7 fixed (in commit TBD): added `assert_tool_invalid_args_envelope_shape` to mcp_conformance_core_tools.rs that fences the FT-MCP-0001 tool-level envelope contract, plus a synthetic unit test that exercises the helper against a reference envelope.

What's still missing: a LIVE test that feeds a real-world payload through `harness.client.call_tool(...)`, slips past framework schema validation, triggers the serde_json::from_value failure at mcp_tools.rs:1025/1216/1387/1940, and lands on the helper assertion.

## Two acceptable outcomes

1. **Slip exists.** Add one regression test per tool (wa.search / wa.get_text / wa.send / wa.wait_for) that calls `assert_tool_invalid_args_envelope_shape` on the real framework response. FT-MCP-0001 fenced live.
2. **No slip exists.** Framework schema validation supersedes the tool-level serde-reject path for all four tools. Remove the defensive branches in mcp_tools.rs (:1025, :1216, :1387, :1940 — and any other tools with the same pattern) as dead code. Update the helper doc-comment to reflect that FT-MCP-0001 is now only emitted by intra-handler paths, not the top-level serde guard.

## Candidate slip payloads to try

- JSON integer > u64::MAX — whether serde_json parses it as f64 and whether the framework's JSON Schema `type: integer` validator catches that depends on which schema lib fastmcp uses.
- Integer-valued float (`1.0`) where a field is `type: integer` — some validators accept, some don't.
- Null for an optional field typed `Option<T>` where T deserialize itself rejects default — unlikely.

## Acceptance Criteria

- Either a merged test that exercises assert_tool_invalid_args_envelope_shape against a live framework response,
- OR a merged diff that removes the dead defensive branches in mcp_tools.rs with the helper doc-comment updated to match.

 {"id":"wa-nu4.3.9.2","title":"Docs: README/quickstart + architecture + CLI/robot/MCP reference","description":"# Task: Documentation for v0.1

## Goal
Make the project approachable for a new contributor and operable for users.

This bead is intentionally self-contained and mirrors PLAN Appendix A + F so contributors don’t need to dig through planning docs.

## Deliverables
### 1) README (operator-first)
- What wa is (WezTerm mux “terminal hypervisor” for agent fleets).
- Quickstart:
  - install wa
  - `wa setup`
  - `wa watch`
  - `wa status`
  - `wa query` / `wa events`
- Safety guarantees:
  - observe vs act split
  - no silent gaps
  - policy-gated sending + approvals

### 2) Architecture doc
- Ingest → Storage (SQLite+FTS5) → Pattern engine → Workflow engine → Robot/MCP.
- Deterministic state via OSC 133.
- Explicit GAP semantics.

### 3) CLI reference (PLAN Appendix A)
Human-oriented commands (rich output by default via `rich_rust`):
- `wa status` — panes + inferred agent state
- `wa watch` — run daemon (foreground/background)
- `wa stop` — stop watcher in workspace safely
- `wa events` — recent/unhandled events
- `wa query` — FTS search
- `wa send` — guarded send to a pane
- `wa reserve` — reserve a pane for exclusive use
- `wa reservations` — list active reservations
- `wa approve` — grant allow-once approvals
- `wa workflow` — run a workflow manually (guarded)
- `wa audit` — audit trail of actions (redacted)
- `wa history` — action timeline + undoability
- `wa undo` — undo supported actions (with confirmation)
- `wa rules` — list/test rule packs, show matching traces
- `wa accounts` — list/refresh accounts, show rotation picks
- `wa setup` — canonical WezTerm configuration (local/remote)
- `wa doctor` — environment checks (wezterm presence, DB health)
- `wa diag bundle` — sanitized diagnostic bundle export
- `wa export` — export slices/history/audit
- `wa web` — optional HTTP server (`fastapi_rust`) if enabled
- `wa tui` — optional TUI (`charmed_rust`) if enabled
- `wa sync` — optional sync (`asupersync`) if enabled

Robot mode commands (stable JSON envelope; token-efficient):
- `wa robot state [--domain <name>] [--agent <type>]`
- `wa robot get-text <pane_id> [--tail N] [--escapes]`
- `wa robot send <pane_id> "<text>" [--no-newline] [--wait-for "<pat>"] [--timeout-secs N]`
- `wa robot wait-for <pane_id> "<pat>" [--timeout-secs N]`
- `wa robot search "<fts query>" [--pane-id <id>] [--since <iso8601>] [--limit N]`
- `wa robot events [--unhandled] [--pane-id <id>] [--type <event>] [--limit N]`
- `wa robot workflow <name> <pane_id> [--force]`
- `wa robot reserve <pane_id> --ttl <secs> --reason <text>`
- `wa robot release <pane_id>`
- `wa robot reservations`
- `wa robot accounts [--service <openai|anthropic|google>]`
- `wa robot accounts refresh [--service <openai|anthropic|google>]`
- `wa robot rules list [--pack <name>]`
- `wa robot rules test "<text>" [--agent <type>]`
- `wa robot quick-start`

### 4) MCP reference (PLAN Appendix A)
Tools (names are short and obvious):
- `wa.state`
- `wa.get_text`
- `wa.send`
- `wa.wait_for`
- `wa.search`
- `wa.events`
- `wa.workflow_run`
- `wa.accounts`
- `wa.accounts_refresh`
- `wa.rules_list`
- `wa.rules_test`
- `wa.reserve`
- `wa.release`
- `wa.reservations`

Resources:
- `wa://panes`
- `wa://events`
- `wa://accounts`
- `wa://workflows`
- `wa://rules`
- `wa://reservations`

### 5) Library integration map (PLAN Appendix F)
| Library | Role in `wa` |
|---------|--------------|
| `cass` (`/dp/coding_agent_session_search`) | Correlation + session archaeology; used in status + workflows |
| `caut` (`/dp/coding_agent_usage_tracker`) | Usage truth + selection; used in accounts + `handle_usage_limits` |
| `rich_rust` | Human-first CLI output (tables/panels/highlight) |
| `charmed_rust` | Optional interactive TUI (pane picker, event feed, transcript viewer) |
| `fastmcp_rust` | MCP tool surface for agent control (mirrors robot mode) |
| `fastapi_rust` | Optional HTTP server for dashboards/webhooks (read-only first) |
| `asupersync` | Remote bootstrap/sync layer (configs, binaries, DB snapshots) |
| `playwright` | Automate device auth flows with persistent profiles |
| `ast-grep` | Structure-aware codebase scans in “unstick agent” workflows |

## Testing / doc correctness
Docs must not drift.

- The quickstart commands shown in README should be validated regularly:
  - either by referencing existing E2E scripts, or
  - by adding a small “docs smoke” CI step that runs the exact commands from the docs in a controlled environment (tracked by `wa-nu4.3.9.9`).

- Robot mode docs should match the schema contract:
  - reference the versioned JSON schemas and keep field names consistent.

## Acceptance Criteria
- A new engineer can get to a running watcher and see events with no oral tradition.
- CLI/robot/MCP references match implemented command surfaces and schemas.
